summary: DeVault argues that GitHub Copilot is trained on copyrighted free software code while disregarding the license terms that govern it, effectively laundering open source code into proprietary software. He contends that the ML model constitutes a derivative work of its GPL and other licensed inputs, meaning those license obligations carry forward to Copilot's outputs. He warns GitHub that the open source community may mount legal challenges, and offers concrete suggestions including opt-in training, license tracking, removing copyleft code from the model, and compensating maintainers. He also advises FOSS developers to leave GitHub and to clarify in their licenses that ML training constitutes derivation, rather than adding non-free restrictions.
key_points: Machine learning's key barriers are datasets and compute, incentivizing ethical shortcuts like ignoring software licenses to cheaply build training data
Even permissive licenses like MIT and BSD require attribution, which Copilot systematically fails to provide, while copyleft licenses impose even stronger obligations on derivative works
The ML model is a derivative work of its inputs because machine learning is pattern recognition and reproduction, not genuine generalization of learning
Copilot enables deliberate laundering of FOSS code: if Microsoft's argument holds, teaching an ML model to regurgitate a function is all that's needed to circumvent any license
FOSS developers should clarify that ML training constitutes derivation under their licenses, rather than adding outright prohibitions that would make the software non-free
money_quotes: GitHub offers plausible deniability through an inscrutable algorithm.
Free software is not an unqualified gift. There are terms for its use and re-use.

If you believe that the force of copyright should protect your proprietary software, then you must agree that it equally protects open source works, despite the inconvenience or cost associated with this truth.
Machine learning is essentially a glorified pattern recognition and reproduction engine, and does not represent a genuine generalization of the learning process.
The only thing which is necessary to legally circumvent a free software license is to teach a machine learning algorithm to regurgitate a function you want to use.
You've invested in building a platform on top of which the open source revolution was built, and leveraging this platform for this move is a deep betrayal of the community's trust.
If it occurs to you that you don't actually pay for GitHub, then you may want to take a moment to consider if the incentives created by that relationship explain this development.
A clause which outright forbids the use of your code for training a machine learning model will make your software non-free, and I do not recommend this approach.
key_insight: GitHub Copilot launders copyleft and permissively licensed code into proprietary software by hiding license-violating derivation behind an inscrutable ML model, and the FOSS community must respond by clarifying that ML training constitutes derivation rather than abandoning software freedom.
